result:
I want to write about silence. Not the absence of sound, but the kind of silence that settles in when you finally stop running.

There’s a silence that comes after a long argument, when the words have all been flung like stones and now lie scattered, useless, on the floor. The air is charged but empty. It’s a silence you can feel in your jaw, clenched tight, and in the hollow of your chest. It’s not peaceful; it’s heavy, waiting for the next tremor. That silence is a suspended verdict.

Then there’s the silence of early morning, just before the world remembers itself. The light is a cool, pale blue, and the only sound might be the distant, lonely call of a single bird. It’s a clean silence, like a blank page. It holds possibility. It doesn’t demand anything of you. You can just exist within it, your thoughts flowing slowly, like water in a deep, still pool.

I think a lot about the silence between people who understand each other perfectly. The silence that sits comfortably in a shared car ride, or over a cup of tea. No need to fill it with chatter. It’s woven from familiarity and trust. It’s a connective silence, a thread made of quiet. You can hear the affection in it.

But the most profound silence I’ve ever known is the one inside a forest of ancient trees. It’s not that there’s no sound—there’s the rustle of leaves, the creak of branches—but those sounds are absorbed into a greater, deeper quiet. It’s the silence of something so immense and old that human noise is just a fleeting scratch on its surface. You stand there, and your own internal noise—your worries, your plans, the buzzing playlist of your memories—just… dissipates. You become part of the quiet. It humbles you. It’s the silence of the earth itself, breathing slowly.

We’re so afraid of silence now. We plug our ears with podcasts, fill our eyes with screens, surround ourselves with a dome of manufactured sound. Silence has become associated with loneliness, with awkwardness, with emptiness. We treat it like a void to be avoided.

But I believe silence is not a void. It’s a container. It’s the space where things actually grow. Ideas clarify. Feelings settle into their true shapes. We hear the voice we’ve been talking over all day—our own.

I’m trying to cultivate a friendship with silence. To not reach for my phone the moment a quiet moment appears. To sit with the silence of my own apartment, to listen to what it might tell me. Sometimes it tells me I’m restless. Sometimes it tells me I’m tired. Sometimes it just lets me be.

It’s in silence that we often encounter the truth. The unvarnished, unspoken truth of how we really feel, of what we really want. Without the noise of opinion, entertainment, or expectation, that truth can rise, quiet and clear, like the moon appearing from behind a cloud.

So I’m writing this in a quiet room. The only sound is the faint click of the keys. And it feels like a kind of prayer. Not to a deity, but to the act of listening. To making room. To the power of the unspoken, the unhurried, the still.

Maybe what we all need, more than anything else, is to find our own unique silence—the one that doesn’t frighten us, but holds us. And to visit it regularly, like a sacred spring, to drink from its clarity.
